Open Visual Studio and go to the `BatteryCollectorGameMode.h`. You are going to set up a power to win variable. If we have a bar to display how much power we need, then we need to know the maximum of the bar to win the game. This is going to be a **protected** variable.

```cpp
/** The power needed to win the game */
UPROPERTY(EditDefaultsOnly, BlueprintReadWrite, Category = "Power",
          Meta = (BlueprintProtected = true))
float PowerToWin;
```

Again, this is on the GameMode because that is where the rules of the game should go, including how to win the game. We are going to make a public function to return what the `PowerToWin` is because this is needed by the HUD.

```cpp
/** Returns the power needed to win - needed for the HUD */
UFUNCTION(BlueprintPure, Category = "Power")
float GetPowerToWin() const;
```

It is a constant because it is not going to change anything about the power, it is just going to return the value. It is going to be a `UFUNCTION()` so we can call it in our Blueprint. Now, implement `GetPowerToWin()` on the source file:

```cpp
float ABatteryCollectorGameMode::GetPowerToWin() const
{
    return PowerToWin;
}
```

We are going to set `PowerToWin` in `BeginPlay()`. We can't set `PowerToWin` in the constructor because our GameMode may not necessarily be constructed after our Character is constructed. We need to base the `PowerToWin` on the Character's `InitialPower`. We are going to do it in `BeginPlay()` because that means everything will already be spawned and registered.

```cpp
virtual void BeginPlay() override;
```

```cpp
void ABatteryCollectorGameMode::BeginPlay()
{
    Super::BeginPlay();

    // set score to beat
    ABatteryCollectorCharacter* MyCharacter = Cast<ABatteryCollectorCharacter>(
        UGameplayStatics::GetPlayerPawn(this, 0));
    if (MyCharacter)
    {
        PowerToWin = (MyCharacter->GetInitialPower()) * 1.25f;
    }
}
```

We will call the parent `BeginPlay()` in case anything happens on the GameMode that we need to make sure still gets called. To test, we are going to multiply and say you need at least **1.25 times your Initial Power** to win the game.

### UMG Dependencies

Before we start making the HUD, we need to change some of the dependencies in our build file. Go to the Solution Explorer and select **`BatteryCollector.Build.cs`**. Add `UMG` to the public dependencies, and add Slate modules as private dependencies:

```csharp
PublicDependencyModuleNames.AddRange(new string[] {
    "Core", "CoreUObject", "Engine", "InputCore",
    "HeadMountedDisplay", "UMG"
});

PrivateDependencyModuleNames.AddRange(new string[] { "Slate", "SlateCore" });
```

UMG requires some of these Slate modules.

### HUD Widget Variables

Go to `BatteryCollectorGameMode.h`:

```cpp
/** The Widget class to use for our HUD screen */
UPROPERTY(EditDefaultsOnly, BlueprintReadWrite, Category = "Power",
          Meta = (BlueprintProtected = true))
TSubclassOf<class UUserWidget> HUDWidgetClass;

/** The instance of the HUD */
UPROPERTY()
class UUserWidget* CurrentWidget;
```

We use a forward declaration because we don't necessarily need to work with the widget yet. The `TSubclassOf` is similar to the `UCLASS()`, but it is restricted to only things which inherit from `UUserWidget`. `CurrentWidget` is marked as a regular `UPROPERTY()` so it is handled properly with garbage collection.

Now, in `BeginPlay()`, after we have set `PowerToWin`, set the widget to use:

```cpp
if (HUDWidgetClass != nullptr)
{
    CurrentWidget = CreateWidget<UUserWidget>(GetWorld(), HUDWidgetClass);
    if (CurrentWidget != nullptr)
    {
        CurrentWidget->AddToViewport();
    }
}
```

You have to include `UserWidget.h` at the top:

```cpp
#include <Blueprint/UserWidget.h>
```

Compile to check that everything is OK. We will go into **UMG UI Designer** in the next section and lay out our UI with our power bar.

---

## Creating the HUD Blueprint

On Unreal Editor, inside Content Drawer, go to the Blueprints folder. Right-click in the Content Drawer, go to **User Interface**, and select **Widget Blueprint**. Select **User Widget**. Call this Blueprint **"BatteryHUD"**. Double-click to open BatteryHUD.

We need a progress bar. In the Palette, drag-and-drop a **Canvas Panel** into the graph. Then, drag a **Progress Bar** into the canvas panel. Click the **Anchors** drop down menu and select the **bottom center**. Set Position X to `0`. Set Position Y to `-100`. Set Size X to `600`. Set Alignment X to `0.5`. This means half of the bar is on the left and half of the bar is on the right.

To set the Percent, we are going to create a binding. Under Progress, go to **Percent** and click the **Bind** drop down. Select **+Create Binding**.

We want the progress bar to be some percentage of the Player Character's Current Power over the GameMode's PowerToWin. Right-click → type and select **"Get Player Character"**. Drag off the Return Value → type "battery" → select **Cast To BatteryCollectorCharacter**. Right-click and select **Convert to pure cast**.

Right-click → type and select **"Get GameMode"**. Drag off Return Value → type "battery" → select **Cast To BatteryCollectorGameMode**. Right-click and select **Convert to pure cast**.

Drag off **As Battery Collector Character** → type "get power" → select **Get Current Power**. Drag off **As Battery Collector GameMode** → type "get power" → select **Get Power To Win**. Divide: drag off Get Current Power Return Value → type `/` → select **float / float**. Plug in **Initial Power** and **Current Power**. Plug the result into the **Return Value** of the Return Node.

Click Compile and click Save.

We need to create a GameMode Blueprint. Go to CollectionLevel. Right-click in the Content Browser and select **Blueprint Class**. Click the drop down → type "battery" → select **BatteryCollectorGameMode**. Name this **"GameMode_BP"**. Double-click to open it. Click the **HUDWidget Class** drop down and select **BatteryHUD**. Click Compile and click Save.

Now, we have to tell CollectionLevel to use the GameMode_BP as our default GameMode. In the main toolbar, click **Edit** and select **Project Settings**. Go to **Maps & Modes**. Click the **Default GameMode** drop down menu and select **GameMode_BP**.

Click Play. We have our progress bar, which you can see ticking down because the GameMode is draining our power. If you collect some batteries, it will fill up the progress bar. In the next section, we are going to start working on the game over and game won states.

---

## Setting Up the Play States

Previously, we set up a power bar so we can see how much power our Character needs to win. Now, we need to set up some code to show what winning and losing really means. We are going to make an **enum** to store the current playing state: playing, game over, or the game has been won. Then, we can start using this state to determine other Blueprint and C++ behavior. Let's go back to our GameMode code. In the `BatteryCollectorGameMode.h` header file, we are going to make a custom enum to store the state of your game. The enum will be above `UCLASS()` in our header file.

```cpp
// enum to store the current state of gameplay
UENUM(BlueprintType)
enum class EBatteryPlayState : uint8
{
    EPlaying,
    EGameOver,
    EWon,
    EUnknown
};
```

We use a macro similar to the `UCLASS()`, `UPROPERTY()` and `UFUNCTION()` macros we used previously. This macro is of the Blueprint type which will allow us to use it later in our HUD_BP. Our 4 states will be `EPlaying`, `EGameOver`, `EWon`, and `EUnknown`. Use the prefix **E** for the naming scheme. `EUnknown` is a default state to handle if things aren't set.

After we have defined the `EBatteryPlayState`, we need a variable to store it. We are going to make the variable **private** because we don't want to be able to change it in any other class. We only want to be able to change it within the code of our GameMode.

```cpp
private:
    /** Keeps track of the current playing state */
    EBatteryPlayState CurrentState;
```

This variable is private, but we want to still read it from our HUD or communicate with the GameMode to set the variable, so we can make public getter and setter functions.

```cpp
/** Returns the current playing state */
UFUNCTION(BlueprintPure, Category = "Power")
EBatteryPlayState GetCurrentState() const;

/** Sets a new playing state */
void SetCurrentState(EBatteryPlayState NewState);
```

You are going to set the state in code only. If you want to change the GameState from a Blueprint, such as in a main menu, you can add a `UFUNCTION()` macro here. Now, make the getter and setter function bodies in `BatteryCollectorGameMode.cpp`:

```cpp
EBatteryPlayState ABatteryCollectorGameMode::GetCurrentState() const
{
    return CurrentState;
}

void ABatteryCollectorGameMode::SetCurrentState(EBatteryPlayState NewState)
{
    CurrentState = NewState;
}
```

You will do more handling of the new play state in the future. For right now, if this function is called, it just changes the current state to the new state that has been requested. When should the GameMode switch to `EPlaying` as a state? On Begin Play. We should make sure to switch the GameState before we do anything else.

```cpp
void ABatteryCollectorGameMode::BeginPlay()
{
    Super::BeginPlay();
    SetCurrentState(EBatteryPlayState::EPlaying);

    // set score to beat
    ABatteryCollectorCharacter* MyCharacter = Cast<ABatteryCollectorCharacter>(
        UGameplayStatics::GetPlayerPawn(this, 0));
    if (MyCharacter)
    {
        PowerToWin = (MyCharacter->GetInitialPower()) * 1.25f;
    }

    if (HUDWidgetClass != nullptr)
    {
        CurrentWidget = CreateWidget<UUserWidget>(GetWorld(), HUDWidgetClass);
        if (CurrentWidget != nullptr)
        {
            CurrentWidget->AddToViewport();
        }
    }
}
```

We want to add whether or not we are in the `EPlaying` state in our `Tick()` function, where we are checking whether or not the CurrentPower level is positive. We don't want to decrease `CurrentPower` if we have already won or lost the game. Let's add some more conditional statements.

```cpp
void ABatteryCollectorGameMode::Tick(float DeltaTime)
{
    Super::Tick(DeltaTime);

    ABatteryCollectorCharacter* MyCharacter = Cast<ABatteryCollectorCharacter>(
        UGameplayStatics::GetPlayerPawn(this, 0));

    if (MyCharacter)
    {
        // If our power is greater than needed to win, set the game's state to Won
        if (MyCharacter->GetCurrentPower() > PowerToWin)
        {
            SetCurrentState(EBatteryPlayState::EWon);
        }
        // if the character's power is positive
        else if (MyCharacter->GetCurrentPower() > 0)
        {
            // decrease the character's power using the decay rate
            MyCharacter->UpdatePower(-DeltaTime * DecayRate * (MyCharacter->GetInitialPower()));
        }
        else
        {
            SetCurrentState(EBatteryPlayState::EGameOver);
        }
    }
}
```

We have 3 states. The game is `EWon` when `CurrentPower` is greater than the `PowerToWin`. If `CurrentPower` is positive, but not greater than the `PowerToWin`, we can start decreasing and decaying `CurrentPower`. If the power is negative, then we will set the state to `EGameOver`. Let's find a way to display the states within our HUD.

Go back to the editor and click Compile. In the Content Browser, go to the Blueprints folder and double-click to open **BatteryHUD**. Select the Designer tab. Put a Text box at the top. In the Palette, drag-and-drop **Text** into the graph. Click the **Anchors** drop down menu and select the **top** of the screen. Set Position X to `0`. Set Position Y to `50`. Check **Size To Content**. Under Alignment, Set X to `0.5` so that it is centered on the screen.

Click the **Bind** drop down menu and select **+Create Binding**. Right-click → type and select **"Get Game Mode"**. Drag off Return Value → type "cast" → select **Cast To BatteryCollectorGameMode**. Connect Get Text 0 to Cast To BatteryCollectorGameMode. Plug the execution output into the Return Node. Drag off **As Battery Collector Game Mode** → type "get state" → select **Get Current State**. Drag off the Return Value enum pin → type "select" → under Struct, click the **Select** node. Connect the Return Value to the Return Value on the Return Node.

For each state, enter the following text:

| State | Text |
|---|---|
| EPlaying | `Press C to collect batteries` |
| EGameOver | `Game Over!` |
| EWon | `You Win!` |

Click Compile and click Save. Go to CollectionLevel. Click Play. You can see at the top it says **"Press C to collect batteries"**. You can see as you collect all the batteries, when you fill up the bar completely, it says **"You Win!"** If you restart the level, and drain the power it says **"Game Over!"** when you lose.

---

## Toggling the Spawn Volumes

Previously, you set up text to display on the screen when the game was playing, when the player won, or when the game is over. There are a few other things we want to have happen for our different playing states. All states have the HUD text, which is all set up and ready for us. We want to have some things happen with the spawn volume:

- **Playing:** spawn volumes are spawning pickups
- **Won:** spawn volumes stop spawning pickups
- **Game Over:** spawn volumes stop spawning pickups, the character collapses, player can no longer input

Throughout this section, we are going to start working with the Spawn Volume class so we can have it work with our new play states. This will include adding a new function to the SpawnVolume class and registering all the spawn volumes in the level with the GameMode. First, go to `SpawnVolume.h` header file. We are going to create a new function to toggle spawning on and off.

```cpp
/** This function toggles whether or not the spawn volume spawns pickups */
UFUNCTION(BlueprintCallable, Category = "Spawning")
void SetSpawningActive(bool bShouldSpawn);
```

This will be a **public** function because we want other classes, like the GameMode, to be able to reach in and access this function. It takes a Boolean for whether or not the spawning volumes should spawn. Make this a `BlueprintCallable` function. This way, at some point in your Blueprint, if you wanted to call whether or not the spawn volume should spawn, you can do that. Go to the `SpawnVolume.cpp` source file. Put the function body above our `SpawnPickup()`:

```cpp
void ASpawnVolume::SetSpawningActive(bool bShouldSpawn)
{
    if (bShouldSpawn)
    {
        // Set the timer on Spawn Pickup
        SpawnDelay = FMath::FRandRange(SpawnDelayRangeLow, SpawnDelayRangeHigh);
        GetWorldTimerManager().SetTimer(SpawnTimer, this,
            &ASpawnVolume::SpawnPickup, SpawnDelay, false);
    }
    else
    {
        // Clear the timer on Spawn Pickup
        GetWorldTimerManager().ClearTimer(SpawnTimer);
    }
}
```

If you remember from earlier, you set up a timer in `BeginPlay()` to determine whether or not our `SpawnPickup` function should be called. Use that same code in `SetSpawningActive`. This will toggle based on the Boolean `bShouldSpawn`. Otherwise if `bShouldSpawn` is false, clear the timer on Spawn Pickup. This means the Spawn Pickup function would no longer be called on a regular interval.

Cut and remove these lines from `BeginPlay()` because we only want the spawn volume to start spawning after the `SetSpawningActive` function has been called. Completely remove this functionality from `BeginPlay()`.

```cpp
// Called when the game starts or when spawned
void ASpawnVolume::BeginPlay()
{
    Super::BeginPlay();
}
```

We are no longer setting the initial timer on BeginPlay because we don't want the spawn volume to start spawning until we call `SetSpawningActive`. We are only going to call `SetSpawningActive` from the GameMode. Open the editor and click Compile. Then, play the game. You will notice your spawn volumes are not spawning your regular pickup, nor the battery pickup because you never called the `SetSpawningActive(true)` function.

### Registering Spawn Volumes in the GameMode

We are going to register all the spawn volumes in the level. You could place 3 or 4 spawn volumes, and they would spawn pickups as long as you set their `WhatToSpawn` variable. We want all of the spawn volumes to be tracked by the GameMode so they can be turned on and off during gameplay. Open the `BatteryCollectorGameMode.h` header file. We are going to store all of the spawn volumes in an array. Let's make a **private** `TArray`. We are really only handling the array on the code side.

```cpp
TArray<class ASpawnVolume*> SpawnVolumeActors;
```

Now that we have `SpawnVolumeActors`, go to the `BatteryCollectorGameMode.cpp` source file. We can now collect all of those Actors in BeginPlay. We need to include the spawn volumes because we need to interact with them directly and start casting.

```cpp
#include "SpawnVolume.h"
```

Go back to BeginPlay and start writing the code to find all the spawn volume Actors. We are going to use another Gameplay Statics function and another array. Use the `UGameplayStatics` function `GetAllActorsOfClass`. This function will go through your entire level. Supply this with the type of class you would like to look for. `StaticClass()` is the argument which the `GetAllActorsOfClass` function takes.

```cpp
void ABatteryCollectorGameMode::BeginPlay()
{
    Super::BeginPlay();

    // find all spawn volume Actors
    TArray<AActor*> FoundActors;
    UGameplayStatics::GetAllActorsOfClass(GetWorld(), ASpawnVolume::StaticClass(), FoundActors);

    for (auto Actor : FoundActors)
    {
        ASpawnVolume* SpawnVolumeActor = Cast<ASpawnVolume>(Actor);
        if (SpawnVolumeActor)
        {
            SpawnVolumeActors.AddUnique(SpawnVolumeActor);
        }
    }

    SetCurrentState(EBatteryPlayState::EPlaying);

    // set score to beat
    ABatteryCollectorCharacter* MyCharacter = Cast<ABatteryCollectorCharacter>(
        UGameplayStatics::GetPlayerPawn(this, 0));
    if (MyCharacter)
    {
        PowerToWin = (MyCharacter->GetInitialPower()) * 1.25f;
    }

    if (HUDWidgetClass != nullptr)
    {
        CurrentWidget = CreateWidget<UUserWidget>(GetWorld(), HUDWidgetClass);
        if (CurrentWidget != nullptr)
        {
            CurrentWidget->AddToViewport();
        }
    }
}
```

We have set up this array of all Actors because the `GetAllActorsOfClass` returns the base Actor type. Then, we are going to work through and cast them. As we go through all of the Actors in `FoundActors`, we are going to try and cast them to spawn volumes. If they are a spawn volume, then we are going to store them in that array. This is the part which meant we had to include `SpawnVolume.h` in this `BatteryCollectorGameMode.cpp` file. We use `AddUnique` so we don't end up with any duplicated spawn volume Actors. Now as soon as the game begins, we are going to find all spawn volume Actors. We will cast the Actors in the array, and if it is a spawn volume then store it in the `SpawnVolumeActor` array.

When we set the current state to `EPlaying`, we will handle our new `EPlaying` state. We need to have the `SpawnVolumeActors` array already populated and ready to talk to. Once we set the current state to `EPlaying`, everything will be stored in this array to work with.

---

## Handling New Play States

We have a few things we want the GameMode to handle while you are playing versus when you have won or lost the game. You have set up all states to have the HUD text. We want the spawn volumes to spawn while you are playing, we also want the spawn volumes to stop spawning when you have won and the spawn volumes to stop spawning when the game is over. At that point, we will have the character ragdoll and we will disable player input.

Previously, you set up the code for the spawn volume to toggle whether it would spawn. You registered all the spawn volumes in the level with our GameMode. Now we can start working on our **state handler**. The state handler will be on our GameMode. The state handler will be a **private** function because we will only call it from within GameMode code.

```cpp
private:
    /** Handle any function calls that rely upon changing the playing state of our game */
    void HandleNewState(EBatteryPlayState NewState);
```

The body of this function is going to be a simple **switch statement**. This is going to switch on the value of `NewState`. We have our different case statements which are all based on our 4 states of the `EBatteryPlayState`. If we have won the game, we want the spawn volumes to be inactive. If you have lost the game, we will still make the spawn volumes inactive and have a little more impact such as block player input and ragdoll the character.

```cpp
void ABatteryCollectorGameMode::HandleNewState(EBatteryPlayState NewState)
{
    switch (NewState)
    {
    // If the game is playing
    case EBatteryPlayState::EPlaying:
    {
        // spawn volumes active
        for (ASpawnVolume* Volume : SpawnVolumeActors)
        {
            Volume->SetSpawningActive(true);
        }
    }
    break;

    // If we've won the game
    case EBatteryPlayState::EWon:
    {
        // spawn volumes inactive
        for (ASpawnVolume* Volume : SpawnVolumeActors)
        {
            Volume->SetSpawningActive(false);
        }
    }
    break;

    // If we've lost the game
    case EBatteryPlayState::EGameOver:
    {
        // spawn volumes inactive
        for (ASpawnVolume* Volume : SpawnVolumeActors)
        {
            Volume->SetSpawningActive(false);
        }

        // block player input
        APlayerController* PlayerController = UGameplayStatics::GetPlayerController(this, 0);
        if (PlayerController)
        {
            PlayerController->SetCinematicMode(true, false, false, true, true);
        }

        // ragdoll the character
        ACharacter* MyCharacter = UGameplayStatics::GetPlayerCharacter(this, 0);
        if (MyCharacter)
        {
            MyCharacter->GetMesh()->SetSimulatePhysics(true);
            MyCharacter->GetMovementComponent()->MovementState.bCanJump = false;
        }
    }
    break;

    // Unknown/default state
    default:
    case EBatteryPlayState::EUnknown:
    {
        // do nothing
    }
    break;
    }
}
```

Now, go to `SetCurrentState` function and add a function call to `HandleNewState`:

```cpp
void ABatteryCollectorGameMode::SetCurrentState(EBatteryPlayState NewState)
{
    // set current state
    CurrentState = NewState;
    // handle the new current state
    HandleNewState(CurrentState);
}
```

This way, once we begin playing, the spawn volumes will all be activated. Once we have won or lost the game, the spawn volumes will all be inactivated.

We are using cinematic mode to disable input. We also ragdoll the character using physics. We need to get the `GetPlayerController`. We are using `UGameplayStatics` again. We are getting the PlayerController. If you had a multiplayer game, you would have to do a little bit more work here. This is a single player game setup. There are several other ways you could do this. This is just a very simple way to illustrate how you could set up disabling the input. We are saying this is true; we are entering cinematic mode (first argument); it is not going to hide the player (second argument); it is not going to affect the HUD (third argument), it is going to affect movement (fourth argument) and it is going to affect turning (fifth and last argument).

To ragdoll the character, we work with the Character using a very similar setup. We don't have to do any specific casting to our type of Character because we are working on the root Character that is included in Unreal with all the movement controls. We have to make sure he is a Character and not a Pawn because Characters have the additional movement set and a Skeletal Mesh. On the Character, we get the mesh and `SetSimulatePhysics` to true, which will make the character ragdoll and collapse after we have lost the game. Finally, on the Character's Movement Component, the movement state is set to false for whether or not it can jump. That is the last bit of code in your state handling.

Go over to the editor and click Compile to test this. We will turn up our Decay Rate, so we can quickly show the `EGameOver` state. Set the **Decay Rate** in `GameMode_BP` to 10 times higher than it was before. Click Compile and click Save. Click Play. The material is changing quickly. The power bar is draining quickly and the character is turning blue. The game will be over pretty soon. My input is disabled, but the character fell through the floor.

Go to the ThirdPersonCPP folder, open the Blueprints folder, and double-click to open `BP_ThirdPersonCharacter`. Under Components, select the **Mesh**. In the Details panel, scroll down to **Collision** and change the **Collision Presets** to **Ragdoll**, so it will collide.

Click Compile and click Save. Go to CollectionLevel to test one more time. Now the character is ragdolled on the floor.

---

## Adding Finishing Touches

This is the final section of this assignment. We are going to wrap up what we have created and polish our level to give you some ideas as jumping off points to keep and take your project to new places. Now that we are done with all the code, we have:

- A **base pickup** which is that gray block that doesn't have any physics on it
- The **battery pickup** which the character can collect and get its power
- The **Character** upgraded with a power level, a collection sphere, and the ability to collect activated pickups
- The upgraded **Game Mode**, which has the Playing state, the Game Over state, and the Won state
- The **spawn volumes** that start or stop spawning
- **Text on our HUD** showing status
- A **HUD** to show both our power level and the descriptive text
- **Spawn volumes** that can create any kind of pickup in our world if it inherits from the Base Pickup class

Let's go back to our project and do a little bit of final polish and tweaking. Again, we have 2 spawn volumes in our level. The first spawn volume spawns our plain grey pickups that don't have physics activated. The other spawn volume spawns the batteries that extend a lightning bolt to the Character. We have 2 spawn volumes in our level, but we could create any number of them because our GameMode collects them and registers the spawn volumes when we begin playing.

For now, we will remove the regular pickup spawn volume because it was just for testing to show our spawn volumes can make any kind of pickup as long as it inherits from the base Pickup class.

In the World Outliner, select SpawnVolume2 and press Delete. We are going to align the other spawn volume with our room from the **Top** view. In the World Outliner, select SpawnVolume1 and move it into the center. Press **R** to use the Scale tool and scale up the spawn volume so it extends over most of the room.

There are a million other things you could add to the game, such as a key press to have your character pull all the pickups in the world towards them and get the electricity out of all of them. It might be a last effort to power up before losing the game. Let's switch the view. Click the View menu and select **Left**. We didn't put a roof on our room, so drag the spawn volume up above the room.

In the World Outliner, select the **PlayerStart** to see where it is relative to the rest of our room. Go back to the Top view to make sure it is centered. Click the View menu and select **Perspective**. Click Play. We have moved some things, so we will have to rebuild lighting again. We can see batteries falling all over the map.

Press Esc. Go to the **`GameMode_BP`** to reset the Decay Rate. It is very easy to tweak and change the values here because we exposed the variables. Test it and see if a Decay Rate of `0.05` is more reasonable. Click Compile and click Save.

Go to CollectionLevel and click Play. Now our power is going down at a much more reasonable rate. We can pick up some of these batteries from all the way across the map. It looks like if we were to keep running around and collecting batteries, we would be able to win the game.

The **"You Win!"** text does not stand out very much right now. Press Esc. Double-click to open **BatteryHUD**. In the Hierarchy, select the **Text Block**. In the Details panel, go to **Appearance** and set the Font size to `48`. We have set it to Size To Content, so it should not impact our overall appearance. Click Compile and click Save.

Click Play. We have our text for how to collect batteries. The batteries are spawning in, so we can start collecting those. You can see our speed and material changing as our power drains and as we collect batteries to power up.

---

## Exercises

Using what you have learned throughout this assignment, try to do the following:

1. Make your Character **glow** as you collect batteries.
2. Show the **number of batteries you have collected** on a counter in the corner.
3. Make it a **nighttime scene** by playing with the sky sphere and the directional light.
4. Create a copy of the original `SM_Battery_Medium` mesh, and change its material to become **red** instead of orange.
5. Create a **new type of Battery**, using the previously created mesh, that has **300 power**.
6. Create a new type of **Spawn Volume**, that has a second type of pickups (special pickups) to respawn and a **probability**. This probability (default value = `0.1`) dictates the chances of the special battery pickup being created when spawning a new battery pickup.
7. Set the previously created battery pickup as the **special type of pickup**.
